text: Overview: Basic vs. Comprehensive Emergency Obstetric Care

Emergency Obstetric Care (EmOC) is the care needed to manage serious complications during pregnancy, childbirth, and the postpartum period. Two levels are defined: Basic Emergency Obstetric Care (BEmOC) provides essential interventions at district hospital or health center level. Comprehensive Emergency Obstetric Care (CEmOC) provides additional interventions including caesarean section and blood transfusion at referral hospital.

items: Parenteral antibiotics (e.g., Ampicillin 1 g IV/IM, Gentamicin 80 mg IM)
Parenteral uterotonics (e.g., Oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV)
Parenteral anticonvulsants (e.g., Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV then 5 g IM Q4H, or Diazepam 10 mg IV)
Manual removal of retained products (e.g., manual removal of placenta, removal of retained products of conception)
Assisted vaginal delivery (e.g., vacuum extraction, forceps delivery)
Surgical capability: Caesarean section for failed progress, fetal distress, transverse lie, placental abruption
Blood transfusion: Access to O-negative blood or type-matched blood

text: On arrival: Assess vital signs (BP, HR, RR, temperature, oxygen saturation). Perform rapid abdominal and vaginal examination. Assess fetal heart rate (FHR) if pregnant. Ask about: bleeding, abdominal pain, contractions, leakage of fluid, loss of consciousness, vaginal discharge, trauma.

items: Severe headache with visual disturbance and BP ≥160/110 mmHg (eclampsia/severe preeclampsia)
Abnormal bleeding (vaginal hemorrhage >500 mL, placental abruption, uterine rupture)
Abdominal pain with peritoneal signs (appendicitis, acute abdomen, ectopic pregnancy)
Fever >38°C with pelvic tenderness (septic abortion, PID, chorioamnionitis)
Loss of consciousness (cerebral malaria, stroke, hypoglycemia, sepsis)
Respiratory distress (heart failure, pulmonary embolism, severe anemia)
Trauma with shock (motor vehicle accident, domestic violence, fall)
Place on examination table with access to pelvis and abdomen
Establish IV access: 2 large-bore cannulas (16-18 gauge) if hemorrhage expected
Start oxygen if SpO2 <90% or respiratory distress
Perform rapid blood tests: blood group and cross-match, hemoglobin/hematocrit, blood glucose, malaria test if endemic
NPO (nothing by mouth) for potential surgery
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring if pregnant
Prepare operating room if hemorrhage, eclampsia, or ruptured uterus

text: PPH is bleeding >500 mL in first 24 hours (or >1000 mL vaginal delivery). Most common cause of maternal death. Causes: uterine atony (80%), retained placenta, genital trauma, coagulopathy.

items: Call for help immediately
Assess bleeding: heavy bleeding = active management
Uterine massage: rub fundus vigorously for 30 seconds
Give uterotonics: Oxytocin 10 IU IM immediately; if IV access, Oxytocin 10 IU IV or 20 IU in 1 L IV fluids at 40 drops/min
Retain placenta? If not delivered after 30 min of contractions, attempt manual removal
IV access: 2 large-bore cannulas, start IV fluids (normal saline, Ringer's lactate)
Assess for shock: Systolic BP <90 mmHg, HR >120, skin cold/clammy, confusion → classified as hemorrhagic shock class III-IV
If shock: Call obstetric team, prepare for theatre, consider transfusion, give Tranexamic acid 1 g IV

text: Eclampsia is seizures in a woman with hypertension and/or protein in urine. Maternal mortality 1-14%. Seizures may precede severe hypertension (pre-eclampsia).

items: Protect airway: position on side (recovery position), do NOT force mouth open, do NOT insert tongue blade
Oxygen: give 100% O2 via face mask
IV access: establish 2 large-bore cannulas
Magnesium sulfate: Loading dose 4 g IV over 5 min (give slowly); then 5 g IM in each buttock (5 g = 10 mL of 50% solution), OR 1 g/hour IV infusion
Monitor: vital signs, respiratory rate (maintain RR ≥16), patellar reflexes (hyperreflexia indicates toxicity)
Deliver baby: expedite delivery by induction/augmentation (if alive) or emergency caesarean section (if fetal distress or no progress)
Avoid ergot alkaloids (ergotamine, methylergonovine) — may trigger severe hypertension

text: Uterine rupture is catastrophic: maternal mortality 5-15%. Risk factors: previous caesarean section, obstructed labor, excessive oxytocin, intrauterine manipulation.
Uterine Rupture Clinical Signs and Management

items: Signs: sudden severe abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, loss of contractions, shock, absent fetal heart rate, fetal body parts palpable per abdomen
Management: IMMEDIATE operative intervention needed
Call obstetric team urgently; prepare theatre
Establish 2 IV lines, type and cross-match blood, start fluid resuscitation
Emergency exploratory laparotomy
Repair rupture if possible; hysterectomy if repair not feasible

text: Anesthesia and Pain Management in EmOC

Regional anesthesia (spinal/epidural) is preferred if patient cooperative and time permits. Agents: Bupivacaine 0.5% 2 mL (heavy) for spinal; Bupivacaine 0.25% 15-20 mL for epidural. General anesthesia if spinal contraindicated: Propofol 2 mg/kg IV, Succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg IV, maintain with Isoflurane or Sevoflurane.
Anesthesia for Manual Removal of Retained Products
Regional anesthesia (spinal 1-1.5 mL 0.5% bupivacaine) is adequate. General anesthesia: Propofol 1-2 mg/kg IV with Nitrous oxide (N2O) 50% and volatile agent. Avoid ergot alkaloids during procedure.
Equipment and Supplies Essential for EmOC

items: Mother: Oxygen delivery system, bag-mask ventilation, emergency airway kit, ECG monitor, pulse oximeter, blood pressure cuff, IV access kit
Baby: Radiant warmer, suction equipment, bag-mask ventilation, emergency medications (epinephrine, naloxone)
Delivery: fetoscope or doppler, partograph for labor monitoring, episiotomy scissors, perineal repair kit
Manual removal: placenta tongs, sponge forceps, sterile gloves, uterine packing materials (merocel sponges)
Vacuum: cup and pump (electric or manual), various cup sizes (60-65 mm soft cup for vertex)
Forceps: Wrigley's or Kielland's forceps with appropriate handles
Caesarean: laparotomy kit, self-retaining retractors, electrocautery, suction, antibiotics
Uterotonics: Oxytocin 10 IU/mL ampoules
Anticonvulsants: Magnesium sulfate 50% vials or powder
Antibiotics: Ampicillin, Gentamicin, Metronidazole for post-operative prophylaxis
Anesthetics: Lidocaine 1%, Bupivacaine 0.25-0.5%, Propofol 1%, Succinylcholine
Cardiovascular: Epinephrine 1:10,000, Hydralazine 5 mg ampoules, Labetalol 5 mg/mL

text: Early identification and transport of high-risk obstetric complications reduce mortality. Establish referral links: health post → health center → district hospital → regional/tertiary hospital. Provide pre-transport stabilization: IV fluids, oxygen, antibiotics, anesthesia if needed. Document condition, treatment given, and monitor during transport.

Track process indicators: Proportion of births attended by skilled attendant, caesarean section rate (should be 5-15%), blood transfusion rate, use of magnesium sulfate for eclampsia. Track outcome indicators: maternal mortality ratio (MMR), perinatal mortality rate (PMR), neonatal mortality rate (NMR). Conduct maternal death reviews to identify gaps and implement corrective actions.
